justification: All rubric criteria met: Both training (weekly batch) and serving (real-time prediction) pipelines are thoroughly designed. The 100ms P99 constraint is central to the architecture — addressed via in-process model serving, pre-computed features in Redis (<5ms), and explicit latency budget allocation (10ms features, 90ms inference). Feature store identified as necessary with Redis backing and training-serving skew prevention. Model versioning via Model Registry with blue-green deployment and canary promotion/rollback. Team size factored throughout — Redis over Feast, in-process serving over TF Serving, phased implementation, explicit 'do NOT build a custom ML platform' guidance. Trade-offs and risks are extensive and specific.

observations.text: Exceptional depth on the 100ms constraint — goes beyond mentioning it to allocating sub-budgets (10ms features, 5ms Redis, remaining for inference) and ruling out remote inference services
Feature store design addresses both online (Redis) and offline (warehouse) paths with shared feature definitions to prevent training-serving skew
Model versioning is well-designed: Model Registry tracks live/candidate/previous with pointer-swap rollback, plus canary deployment with metric-based promotion
Team size awareness is woven throughout, not just a footnote — Redis over Feast, cron over Airflow, phased build, 2+2 team split recommendation
Identifies training-serving skew as the #1 ML system failure mode and designs specifically against it with shared feature definitions
Phased implementation plan is practical and gets a working system to production in 2-3 weeks before adding automation
Strong model choice guidance — LightGBM/XGBoost for tabular data with clear rationale for why deep learning is inappropriate here

{
  "executive_summary": "Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service with a clear separation between the serving path (latency-critical, called at checkout) and the training path (batch, weekly). trials.justification: All rubric criteria met: Both training (weekly batch) and serving (real-time prediction service) pipelines are fully designed with clear component boundaries. 100ms P99 constraint is thoroughly addressed — pre-computed features in Redis (<10ms fetch), in-process model inference (<20ms target), explicit guidance against real-time feature computation. Feature store identified as necessary with online/offline split. Model versioning via Model Registry with blue/green deployment and instant rollback via Deployment Controller. Team size factored throughout — manual rollback, managed services recommendation, XGBoost over complex models, explicit team workload split (2 ML + 2 platform). Trade-offs and risks are extensive and well-reasoned.

observations.text: Exceptional latency budget breakdown — explicitly allocates <10ms for feature fetch and <20ms for inference, showing the 100ms budget is achievable with margin
Feature store design includes both online (Redis) and offline (Parquet/S3) tiers with clear rationale for training-serving skew prevention
Blue/green model deployment with manual rollback is well-justified for a 4-person team — explicitly considered and rejected canary/A/B as premature complexity
Identifies training-serving skew as critical risk with concrete mitigation (shared feature definition codebase + integration tests)
Stale feature risk addressed with TTLs, freshness monitoring, and degraded-prediction fallback
Phased implementation plan is realistic and starts with a heuristic model to validate serving infrastructure before ML complexity
Model choice (XGBoost/LightGBM) is well-justified for tabular data with few features and fits the latency constraint
Fallback path for Redis failure ensures checkout doesn't break when ML system is down
Could have been more explicit about model artifact size constraints imposed by the latency budget — mentioned sub-5ms inference for XGBoost but didn't quantify the ceiling for model complexity

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service serving predictions at P99 <100ms during checkout. The design separates the hot path (feature lookup + model inference) from the cold path (training pipeline + model registry) to independently optimize latency and throughput. A lightweight feature store bridges both paths. Model versioning with instant rollback is achieved via a blue/green model deployment pattern behind a routing layer. Designed for a 4-perso

trials.justification: All rubric criteria met: Both training (weekly batch) and serving (Inference Service + Feature Store) pipelines are fully designed with clear components and interfaces. 100ms P99 constraint is directly addressed — model complexity is constrained (LightGBM for sub-10ms inference), feature store provides O(1) lookups, and a latency benchmark gate prevents deploying slow models. Feature Store is identified as necessary with online/offline split. Model versioning via Model Registry with blue/green tagging and instant rollback is fully designed. Team size (4 people) is factored throughout — managed services recommended, shadow scoring over full A/B testing, explicit warning against Kubeflow/Airflow/Feast/Seldon stack. Trade-offs and risks are comprehensive and well-reasoned.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough two-path architecture with clear component boundaries, interfaces, and data flow
100ms P99 addressed at multiple levels: model choice (LightGBM sub-10ms), feature store (sub-5ms), and deployment gate (P99 < 50ms benchmark to leave headroom)
Feature store designed with online/offline split and explicit rationale for training-serving parity
Model versioning with active/candidate/rollback tags and blue/green deployment fully specified
Team size constraints deeply integrated — recommends MLflow + FastAPI + Redis over heavy ML platforms, shadow scoring over A/B testing, weekly batch over continuous learning
Identifies feature staleness and model drift risks explicitly, with concrete mitigations
Phased implementation plan that de-risks latency first by deploying a heuristic model behind the API contract
Includes Feature Store failure mode (SPOF on serving path) with cache fallback and circuit breaker — goes beyond rubric requirements

trials.agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Architecture for a real-time ML delivery time estimation service serving predictions at P99 < 100ms during checkout. The design separates the hot prediction path (a lean inference service with pre-loaded models) from the cold training path (weekly batch retraining pipeline). Model versioning with instant rollback is achieved via a model registry with blue/green deployment. Given a 4-person team, the architecture favors managed services and proven patterns over custom infras
